career.notes: Most districts post openings on rolling basis. Snohomish has confirmed history positions with salary range $75-149k. Seattle Academy has one specific long-term sub history role. Other districts require checking their application portals regularly for new postings. All require WA state teaching certificate with appropriate endorsements.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded its 2025-26 season auditions, but invites singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the mailing list for August 2026 all-voice-part auditions. Rehearsals are Tuesday evenings 6:30-9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave). Dues $75/year with scholarships available. Requirements include music-reading skills and prior singing or instrumental experience. Small ensembles (Vox, Chroma, Orpheon) hold additional auditions after the full choir season begins.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries offers weekly Toddler Storytime at Lynnwood Library (19200 44th Ave W) on Mondays 9:30-10:00 am starting May 4, featuring stories, music, and movement for ages 18-36 months followed by play time. Edmonds Library (650 Main St) lists Preschool Storytime Tuesdays 10:00-10:45 am and Baby Storytime on multiple days. Both branches provide free storytimes with songs, rhymes, and creative activities designed to build early literacy skills.

global_news.findings: Ukraine has accused Israel of receiving shipments of grain 'stolen' by Russia from occupied Ukrainian territory. President Zelensky stated that a vessel carrying grain 'stolen by Russia' arrived at Haifa port and was preparing to unload. Israel's Foreign Minister Gideon Saar rejected what he called 'Twitter diplomacy' and said Ukraine had not provided evidence. The EU condemned actions that help fund Russia's war effort and indicated readiness to target entities facilitating such shipments.

President Trump stated at a White House state dinner for King Charles that Britain's monarch agrees with him that Iran should not have nuclear weapons. Trump said 'we're never going to let that opponent have a nuclear weapon' and claimed Charles agrees with him 'even more than I do.' The comments came during Charles' four-day US state visit, where the king addressed Congress about democratic values but did not directly mention the Iran conflict.

The US-Iran conflict has reached a deadlock two months after the war began, with peace talks stalled over control of the Strait of Hormuz and Iran's nuclear program. Iran has proposed opening the strait if the US lifts its naval blockade but wants to postpone nuclear negotiations. The UAE announced it is leaving OPEC after nearly 60 years amid the turmoil. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Iran's leadership is 'deeply fractured' and that the US cannot tolerate Iran controlling the strategic waterway.

King Charles III and Queen Camilla began their historic four-day US state visit, arriving to a military welcome. Charles addressed Congress on Tuesday, where he emphasized defending democratic values following the assassination attempt on President Trump. The visit comes amid tense US-UK relations over the Iran conflict, with Trump having criticized British Prime Minister Keir Starmer for insufficient support in prosecuting the war against Iran.

The United States and Iran clashed at the United Nations over Tehran's selection as a vice president for the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty conference. The US called Iran's selection an 'affront' to the NPT's credibility, citing Iran's refusal to cooperate with the UN nuclear watchdog. Iran rejected the criticism as 'baseless and politically motivated,' accusing the US of hypocrisy given its own nuclear arsenal. The confrontation highlights ongoing tensions over Iran's nuclear program amid the broader conflict.

intellectual_journals.findings: Maria Popova's essay on Fanny Wright reveals the forgotten Scottish-American radical who pioneered scientific thinking and free love in early 19th-century America. Wright, who inspired young Walt Whitman through her radical newspaper The Free Enquirer, was orphaned at age three and raised by an aunt who introduced her to French materialist philosophy. At sixteen, she gained access to the University of Glasgow's library through a great-uncle who held Adam Smith's former chair, immersing herself in American democratic ideals. Her 1813 novel "A Few Days in Athens" advanced Epicurean atomic realis [TRUNCATED]

A comprehensive investigation by ProPublica and FRONTLINE reveals that Trump's immigration enforcement has led to over 300 arrests of protesters and bystanders during immigration sweeps, with cases repeatedly collapsing under legal scrutiny. Federal agents have routinely accused dissenters of domestic terrorism, using conspiracy statutes typically reserved for drug traffickers. In Los Angeles, Marine veteran Alejandro Orellana was arrested in a heavily publicized raid for distributing water to protesters, charged under federal conspiracy laws, only to have the case dismissed within weeks due t [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy's essay explores Marcel Proust's defense of literature as a key to personal freedom, contrasting his views with John Ruskin's moralizing approach to reading. Proust argued that books serve not as instruments of moral betterment but as unique conduits for accessing the minds of history's greatest thinkers, preserving past sensations and making time tangible through memory. The essay examines Proust's 1905 essay "On Reading," written as a preface to Ruskin's "Sesame and Lilies," where Proust rejected Ruskin's utilitarian view of reading as mere social advancement. Instead, Proust ch [TRUNCATED]

Francine Prose's review of two contemporary novels - Lee Clay Johnson's "Bloodline" and Carl Hiaasen's "Fever Beach" - examines how fiction can address our current political moment without sacrificing narrative pleasure. Johnson's novel follows Winston Alcorn, a former used car salesman who deliberately saws off his hand in an insurance scam, believing himself descended from Confederate General John Hunt Morgan. The character's transformation into "Wins-a-ton" represents a grotesque embodiment of historical grievance and white supremacist fantasy. Hiaasen's South Florida satire similarly uses  [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Damiano Migliorini's "Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk" (2022) advances a rigorous analytic model where divine persons are conceived as "subsisting relations" rather than substances, using trinitarian doctrine as an ontological key to resolve the aporia between divine omniscience and human freedom. Christopher Isabelle's "Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field" (2026) argues that dyadic structures are insufficient for emergence and meaning, proposing an irreducible third term—a "relational field" that must be actively maintained through tension between poles or intelligibility itself disappears. Kenneth Hammat's "Consciousness Between Fact and Value: A Triadic Neurophenomenology" (February 2026) integrates neuroscience with phenomenology through three irreducible domains—object (material), idea (mental), and relation (axiological)—unified through consciousness, introducing a cross-domain classification [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 papers push autonomous research from lab demos to hardened production systems. InternAgent-1.5 (arXiv:2602.08990) is the first end-to-end framework that unifies computational modeling and wet-lab experimentation inside a single agentic loop; it scored best-in-class on GAIA, HLE, GPQA and FrontierScience while autonomously discovering new ML algorithms and running full biology/physics experiments. Mimosa (arXiv:2603.28986) attacks the brittleness of fixed workflows: a meta-orchestrator uses the new Model Context Protocol to discover tools on the fly, then spins up task-specific multi-agent topologies that evolve via experimental feedback; on ScienceAgentBench it beats static multi-agent baselines with a 43 % success rate. Google’s MARS (arXiv:2602.02660) focuses on cost-aware AI-research automation: a budget-sensitive Monte-Carlo tree search plans experiments, modular code generation keeps GPU hours explicit, and a reflective memory bank distills “Aha!” insights across branch [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Three key documents: 1) House Oversight Committee held a joint hearing 13 Nov 2024 titled “Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena: Exposing the Truth” with Elizondo, Gallaudet, Gold & Shellenberger testifying; full transcript posted 3 Feb 2025. 2) IBTimes reports that astrophysicist Dr Eric Davis has already briefed multiple congressional committees in SCIFs about UFO crash-retrieval and Non-Human Intelligence programs, but public release is blocked by presidential-level NDAs. 3) Rep. Anna Paulina Luna’s Task Force continues pressing the Department of War in 2025-26 for withheld UAP videos, citing inadequate AARO transparency and national-security risks.

newyorker.title: The New Normal
newyorker.dek: In a city of eight million, the abnormal has become routine.
newyorker.text:
The morning after the White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooting, New Yorkers woke to a city that felt both familiar and strange. At the Times Square subway station, a man in a tuxedo slept on a bench, his bow tie askew, clutching a program from the dinner. Above ground, tourists posed for selfies with NYPD officers who had been deployed in unprecedented numbers overnight. “It’s like the city decided to dress up for its own funeral,” one officer told me, adjusting his tactical vest.

At the Plaza Hotel, where several attendees had decamped after the dinner, the lobby buzzed with a peculiar energy. A former cabinet secretary, still in his formal attire, argued with a concierge about the availability of a private jet. “I don’t care if it’s a puddle-jumper,” he said. “I need to be in California by noon.” Nearby, a network anchor practiced her somber voice for the evening broadcast, repeatedly saying, “The nation mourns,” while checking her reflection in a gilded mirror.

The city’s resilience was on display in unexpected ways. In Central Park, a group of street performers incorporated the previous night’s chaos into their routine, juggling rubber chickens while shouting, “Duck! It’s a metaphor!” A hot-dog vendor near Columbus Circle offered a “Survival Special”—two dogs and a can of soda for five dollars—claiming it was “what the Secret Service eats when they’re off duty.”

By afternoon, the abnormal had settled into a kind of rhythm. The stock market dipped, then rebounded. Broadway shows went on as scheduled, though with heightened security. At the 9/11 Memorial, tourists laid flowers not just for the victims of 2001 but also for the would-be assassin, a gesture that confused even the guides. “People need rituals,” one said, shrugging. “Even when they don’t make sense.”

As dusk fell, the city’s lights flickered on, defiant and bright. In Brooklyn, a bar hosted a “Post-Assassination Trivia Night,” where teams competed to name every president who had survived an attempt on his life. The winning team, calling themselves “The Bulletproofs,” celebrated with shots of tequila. “We’re all just improvising now,” the bartender told me, pouring another round. “But hey, that’s New York.”

literary_pick.title: Wolf Hall
literary_pick.author: Hilary Mantel
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2009 Man Booker Prize, Hilary Mantel's Wolf Hall is widely regarded as one of the greatest historical novels of the 21st century. The book reimagines the rise of Thomas Cromwell at the court of Henry VIII, offering a fresh perspective on Tudor England through sensuously vivid prose and deep exploration of power, fate, and fortune. Critics have praised it as 'the best novel of the century' with 'language as fresh as new paint,' noting how Mantel makes history feel immediate and relevant to our own era.
